data.title: European Stocks Rally
data.description:
Major European bourses rallied on Thursday, with both the STOXX 50 and STOXX 600 surging nearly 5%, as markets rebounded sharply from the previous session's sell-off and tracked strong gains in Asia.

The STOXX 600 was on pace for its biggest one-day jump since March 2020 after President Trump announced a 90-day suspension of reciprocal tariffs for most U.S. trade partners - excluding China - to allow for renewed negotiations.

In contrast, tariffs on Chinese goods were hiked to 125%.

The US now maintains a 10% baseline tariff on imports from all countries, including the EU, though key exemptions remain in place for semiconductors, copper, lumber, pharmaceuticals, precious metals, energy, critical minerals, and automobiles and auto parts.

Banking, technology, and industrial stocks led Thursday’s rally.

data.title: Soybeans rise after Trump's tariff U-turn
data.description:
Chicago soybean futures extended gains on Thursday after shooting up 2% in the previous session when U.S. President Donald Trump said he would pause tariff increases on dozens of countries to allow time for trade negotiations.

Trump's stunning U-turn sent equities and oil prices sharply higher and raised hopes that some disruption of agricultural trade can be avoided, despite his decision to raise tariffs again on China, the biggest buyer of U.S. farm goods.

Corn futures rose and wheat edged lower.

Traders feared that more countries would follow China and block U.S. imports, reducing demand for U.S. crops and putting downward pressure on Chicago prices, said Commonwealth Bank analyst Dennis Voznesenski.

But so far few have done so and Trump's de-escalation reduces the risk that they will, he said.

"Now that Trump's pushed back the larger tariffs, the market is less concerned that those countries will retaliate," he said.

The most-active soybean contract on the Chicago Board of Trade (CBOT) was up 0.2% at $10.15 a bushel at 0642 GMT. CBOT corn rose 0.2% to $4.74-3/4 a bushel and wheat was down 0.1% at $5.42 a bushel.

All three contracts are now at or above their levels before Trump announced his barrage of tariffs last week, helped partly by a weaker U.S. dollar.

Damage has been done to U.S. farm exports, however. Asian buyers are reducing purchases and China, which typically absorbs around half of U.S. soybean exports, has imposed counter-tariffs big enough to halt all shipments.

Firm U.S. cash markets for soybeans are supporting prices. Farmers have been reluctant to book fresh sales at current prices, forcing buyers to raise their bids to get supplies, traders say.

The market is meanwhile waiting for a monthly supply and demand report due from the U.S. Department of Agriculture on Thursday that could shift prices.

data.title: BSP Delivers 25bps Rate Cut as Expected
data.description:
The Central Bank of the Philippines cut its benchmark interest rate by 25bps to 5.5% at its April 2025 policy meeting, in line with market expectations.

The move was supported by easing inflation, with consumer prices rising just 1.8% year-on-year in March — the slowest pace since May 2020 and below the central bank’s 2% to 4% target range.

The rate cut also aimed to support the economy amid rising global trade tensions, which have sparked concerns over a broader economic slowdown.

data.title: CleanTech Lithium Names New CEO
data.description:
CleanTech Lithium appointed Ignacio Mehech as its new chief executive officer.

The lithium exploration and development company said Thursday that Mehech previously served as country manager for battery-grade lithium producer Albemarle in Chile.

data.title: Egypt's headline inflation increases to 13.6% in March
data.description:
Egypt's annual urban consumer price inflation accelerated to 13.6% in March from 12.8% in February, higher than analysts had expected, data from the state statistics agency CAPMAS showed on Thursday.

The median forecast of analysts polled by Reuters was for annual urban consumer inflation to have slipped to 12.6%.

Month on month, prices were 1.6% higher in March than in February. Food and beverage prices climbed 3.5% in March.

Annually, food and beverage prices rose by 6.6%, up from 3.7% in February.

Annual urban inflation had plunged to 12.8% in February from 24.0% in January, largely the result of a statistical base effect, as exceptionally fast price increases of the past two years were no longer reflected in the statistics, analysts said.

Inflation began soaring after the Russian invasion of Ukraine in early 2022, prompting foreign investors to withdraw billions of dollars from Egyptian treasury markets. The Egyptian government turned to printing pounds to help plug its budget deficit.

Headline inflation reached a record high of 38.0% in September 2023.

M2 money supply has continued to expand, reaching an all-time high of 33.9% in the year to end-February, central bank data showed.

In March of last year, Egypt devalued its currency, raised interest rates by 600 basis points and signed an $8 billion financial support package with the International Monetary Fund, helping to bring its finances under control.

data.title: AAC Technologies Completes Buyback; Launches New HK$1.2 Billion Program
data.description:
AAC Technologies completed its automatic share buyback program, repurchasing a total of 19,676,000 shares for around HK$778 million through on-market buybacks.

The shares represent 1.6417% of the miniature acoustic components maker's issued share capital.

The firm's board, on the same day, authorized a new round of buybacks from the open market worth up to HK$1.2 billion in the next 12 to 18 months.

data.title: Benzene prices witness a drop in Asia
data.description: Benzene prices fell in Asia on Wednesday.An industry source in Asia informed a Polymerupdate team member, "Prices were assessed lower on the back of a bearish purchase pulse in the Asian markets."On Wednesday, FOB Korea benzene prices were assessed at the USD 700-710/mt levels, a drop of USD (-15/mt) from Tuesday's assessed levels. CFR China benzene prices on Wednesday were assessed at the USD 720-730/mt levels, a day on day decline of USD (-15/mt).Meanwhile, downstream SM prices on Wednesday were assessed at the USD 850-860/mt FOB Korea levels, down USD (-20/mt) from Tuesday.

data.title: A Singaporean producer offers Low density polyethylene (LDPE Coating) in Malaysia
data.description: A Polymerupdate source in Malaysia informed that a producer from Singapore is heard to have offered its LDPE Coating grade at the USD 1450/mt levels.This offer is for April 2025 shipment on CFR Malaysia Main port basis.

data.title: A Chinese producer offers Polypropylene (PP Impact Copolymer) in Malaysia
data.description: A Polymerupdate source in Malaysia stated that a producer from China is heard to have announced its offer for PP Impact Copolymer grade at the USD 1020/mt levels.This offer is for April 2025 shipment on CFR Malaysia Main port basis.

data.title: SC Agrotech's CFO Resigns
data.description: Dairy producer SC Agrotech said its chief financial officer Amit Sehgal has resigned from the company with effect from April 8, according to a Thursday filing to the BSE.

data.title: Avalon Technologies to Acquire 4% Stake in Zepco Technologies
data.description:
Electronic manufacturing services company Avalon Technologies agreed to acquire a 4% stake in Zepco Technologies, according to a Thursday filing to the Indian stock exchanges.

Zepco manufactures and sells motors for various industries including drones, electric vehicles and defense.

Avalon plans to invest in Zepco through the subscription of equity shares and compulsorily convertible preference shares at 306.55 rupees per security, inclusive of premium, with a face value of 10 rupees.

The investment is part of proposed wider business partnership between the two companies. They plan to use Zepco's technology and design expertise and Avalon's experience to explore new opportunities, develop manufacturing capabilities, and build customer relationships.

The transaction is expected to be completed in three to four weeks.

data.title: News Highlights: Top Global Markets News of the Day - Thursday at 3 AM ET
data.description:
Asian Markets Power Higher After Trump's About-Face on Tariffs

Asian stock markets powered higher on Thursday, following President Trump's decision to pause steep tariffs on a swath of countries that had been put into effect just hours before.

China's Consumer Prices Continue to Signal Weakness

Chinese consumer prices continued to fall in March, pointing to persistent disinflationary pressure and adding urgency to Beijing's efforts to stimulate consumption as steep U.S. tariffs kick in.

Why Trump Blinked on Tariffs Just Hours After They Went Into Effect

Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent helped persuade the president to take some time and negotiate with trading partners in the face of pressure from business leaders.

Break in Trade Storm Still Leaves Job for RBA

Australia's central bank has the ammunition to respond and should use it, given that China and trading partners elsewhere in Asia are still in the crosshairs of Trump's tariffs.

Trump Transforms Tariff War Into High-Stakes Showdown With China

The clash locks the world's two largest economies in an extraordinary conflict without any immediate exits.

Trump tariff pause calms economists for now - but a recession is 'not off the table' yet

Goldman now sees no recession, but keeps odds of serious downturn at just under 50%

Fed Officials Flagged Risks of 'More Persistent' Inflation From Tariffs

Some policymakers at last month's meeting warned of difficult trade-offs that loom if inflation rises and hiring deteriorates.

Bond Market Calms as 10-Year Treasury Auction Sees 'Very Strong' Demand

Worries of a so-called busted sale had been rising.

EU Member States Approve Response to U.S. Steel Tariffs

European Union members approved an initial list of U.S. goods that will be subject to tariffs, giving the bloc power to impose levies on some products as early as next week.

ECB Has Many Instruments It Can Deploy, Cipollone Says

The European Central Bank has many instruments it can deploy in the event that economic disruption and sharp falls in financial markets threaten to turn into a crisis, a senior official said Wednesday.

data.title: News Highlights: Top Company News of the Day - Thursday at 3 AM ET
data.description:
IPOs Put On Ice By Tariff Uncertainty, EY Warns

Some companies planning to list this year, especially those aiming at going public in the first quarter, have delayed their IPOs to as late as early 2026.

Apple Stock Jumps on Tariff News, Hope for Exemption

President Donald Trump is raising China's tariff rate to 125% but also said he is considering excluding imports by some companies.

Ford Stock Soars. There Is Still a Huge Problem.

Bernstein analyst Daniel Roeska downgraded Ford Motor stock to Sell from Hold. He had downgraded GM to Sell from Hold on Monday.

Costco's March Was Strong. Tariff Uncertainty Didn't Stop Consumers.

Sales rose by 8.6% year over year in March.

Inside Walmart's Meeting With Investors on a Day of Tariff Turmoil

As global markets gyrated over tariffs, Walmart executives gathered with their biggest investors in Dallas this week and worked to project calm.

Sweepstakes Company Publishers Clearing House Files for Bankruptcy

The direct-mail company's Prize Patrol gained fame delivering oversize checks with TV cameras running.

EQT Stake Sale Values Swedish Software Developer IFS at Over $16 Billion

The roughly $3.29 billion deal gives Hg control of the company alongside EQT.

Accounting Firms Baker Tilly and Moss Adams Near Merger

Moss Adams would become partly owned by private equity through the deal, which would create a combined firm with more than $3 billion in annual revenue.

Constellation Brands to Divest Cheaper Wine Brands Amid Organizational Review

Alcohol heavyweight Constellation Brands will undergo a review of its organizational structuring that is expected to save the company more than $200 million by 2028.

Harley-Davidson Director Resigns Citing 'Grave Concerns' About Company

Jared Dourdeville of the New York investment firm H Partners said the company had suffered from "cultural depletion."

data.title: European Bank Shares Rebound After Trump's Tariff PauseMarket Talk
data.description: Shares in European banks jumped at market open on Thursday, along with the wider market, as investors breathed a sigh of relief after President Trump instituted a 90-day pause on the so-called reciprocal tariffs. The Stoxx 600 banks index opened around 6% higher, recovering ground lost in the past few sessions as the announcement of sweeping levies triggered fears of a global recession. The wider Stoxx 600 was up roughly 1% in opening exchanges. While banks aren't in the direct line of fire on tariffs, they would suffer from slower economic growth with customers and corporates dialing back on borrowing, finding it harder to pay back debts. Moribund economies also put downward pressure on interest rates and uncertainty slows dealmaking, eating into lenders' sources of revenue. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

data.title: Creaking US bond market signals danger still lurks: McGeever
data.description:
Global markets are breathing a huge sigh of relief following U.S. president Donald Trump's surprise announcement of a 90-day hiatus in much of his tariff agenda, but it would be premature to think the dangers posed by the U.S. bond market dysfunction this week have suddenly disappeared.

When financial crises migrate to Treasuries, regulators start to get very nervous, as stress here can lead to large, rapid shifts in prices that suddenly clog up market 'plumbing' and inhibit its functioning, requiring the Federal Reserve to step in.

And cracks started to appear this week in the $29 trillion Treasury market, the bedrock of the global financial system and the benchmark for trillions of dollars of loans globally, as investors dumped huge amounts of long-dated bonds.

The moves in Treasuries and swap spreads were extraordinary, in some cases historic. The 30-year swap spread briefly widened through -100 basis points on Tuesday, its most negative since the pandemic. The 10-year yield at one point was up more than 40 bps and on course for its biggest weekly rise since 2001. And the 30-year yield gapped up 60 bps, on track for its biggest weekly rise since 1981.

These moves were not reflective of a liquid, well-functioning market, especially as they came in a deep 'risk off' environment, which should, in theory, have been spurring huge safe-haven inflows.

Indeed, the speed and scale of liquidation in the scramble to raise cash, limit losses and meet margin calls had echoes of the early days of the pandemic crisis five years ago. Tensions had cooled a bit even before Trump's announcement on Wednesday, but yields remain elevated, and the trade war is far from over – just ask China – so these bond tremors could easily erupt again.

MARKET FUNCTIONING

Federal Reserve officials will thus continue to watch keenly for any warning signs of danger ahead, including evaporating liquidity, plummeting demand, widening bid-offer spreads and 'gapping' yields in certain parts of the curve.

The Fed will be on the lookout for a 'buyers' strike', whether that's domestic or foreign investors, something that may have been brewing this week before Trump's tariff surprise. Bank of America's Meghan Swiber warned earlier on Wednesday that further deleveraging and liquidation risked a downward spiral that could only be halted by official intervention.

What would the Fed's options be in this scenario? Interest rate cuts would be highly unlikely, as they would not be especially helpful in resolving a market dislocation issue.

The central bank has amassed an array of liquidity backstops and lending facilities since 2008, such as the overnight reverse repo program, standing repo facility, dollar swap lines with foreign central banks, and the discount window. These tools could all be sharpened, deployed or extended as policymakers see fit.

The Fed could also announce a temporary or permanent exemption for Treasuries in banks' Supplementary Leverage Ratio calculations, which would ease bond market strains and encourage banks to keep the lending taps open. It could also end its balance sheet runoff, or quantitative tightening program, earlier than planned.

And in extremis, if the market really froze like it did in 2020 and 2008, the Fed could go full circle and start buying bonds again.

That's the ultimate bazooka, which the Fed will be loath to dust down and fire. It would prefer not to be called upon at all, and maybe Trump's tariff pause means it won't have to. But more volatile weeks like this one in Treasuries, and it may have no choice.

(The opinions expressed here are those of the author, a columnist for Reuters.)

data.title: Anglo American Shares Up 11% in Opening Trade
On April 10, 2025, the Helsinki Court of Appeal issued a judgment in the securities market criminal case of Nokian Tyres plc
data.description:
Nokian Tyres

Nokian Tyres plc Stock Exchange Release April 10, 2025, at 10:15 a.m. EEST

The case heard in the Court of Appeal was related to the events of 2015-2016. According to the charges, the company's Board of Directors and President and CEO at the time had failed to disclose information affecting the value of the company's securities in December 2015. In addition, according to the charges, four of the company's employees had made insider transactions with the company's stock options at the end of 2015. The District Court had dismissed all charges in 2022.

The Court of Appeal dismissed the charges against the company's former board members Hille Korhonen, Raimo Lind, Inka Mero, Hannu Penttilä and Petteri Walldén. The Court of Appeal sentenced the company's former President and CEO Ari Lehtoranta to a fine for a securities market information offence. Three of the company's employees were sentenced to fines and one employee to 4 months and 15 days of suspended imprisonment for misuse of inside information. The company was ordered to pay a corporate fine of EUR 50,000. The company was not found to have engaged in malpractices in magazine tests as alleged in the charges. 

"We are surprised by the judgment of the Court of Appeal. We will go through the verdict carefully and assess separately whether there is reason to apply for leave to appeal to the Supreme Court," says Kai Kotiranta, the company's attorney-at-law.

The judgment of the Court of Appeal is not final. The company does not comment on the matter further.

Nokian Tyres plc

media@nokiantyres.com

investors@nokiantyres.com

Nokian Tyres' purpose is to make the world safer by reinventing tires, and how they are made, over and over again - a safer place to drive, work and live now and for generations to come. Inspired by our northern heritage, we develop and manufacture premium tires for passenger cars, trucks and heavy machinery with sustainability at the heart of all our operations. Our Vianor chain provides tire and car services. We are some 3,800 people with net sales of EUR 1.3 billion in 2024, and together we lead the journey to smarter driving for people and for businesses. Nokian Tyres is listed on Nasdaq Helsinki. Further information: company.nokiantyres.com

data.title: Derayah REIT Fund Secures SAR750 Million Bank Facility from Alinma Bank
data.description:
Derayah REIT Fund (SASE:4339) secured a 750 million-Saudi-riyal sharia-compliant financing facility from Alinma Bank (SASE:1150), according to a Thursday release.

The five-year deal, signed by Derayah Financial (SASE:4084), is intended to refinance the real estate investment traded fund's current obligations amounting to 603 million riyals.

The group said the remaining amount in the facility could be used to buy real estate assets.

data.title: Market Chatter: Australia Rejects China's Call to "Join Hands" Against US Tariffs
data.description:
Australia turned down China's proposal to collaborate against US tariffs as it looks at diversifying its international trade and reducing its reliance on China, Reuters reported Thursday, citing Deputy Prime Minister Richard Marles during an interview with Sky News.

Xiao Qian, the Chinese ambassador to Australia, proposed in an opinion column in The Age that the Australian government, along with the international community, should "join hands" with Beijing to defend its global trade, the report said.

The statement was made after US President Donald Trump announced that he would raise tariffs against Chinese goods to 125% from 104%, the report said.

Disclaimer

Market Chatter news is derived from conversations with market professionals globally. This information is believed to be from reliable sources but may include rumor and speculation. Accuracy is not guaranteed.

data.title: South African rand regains some ground after US pauses tariffs
data.description:
South Africa's rand firmed in early trade on Thursday, recouping some of its recent loss as investors took a breather after U.S. President Donald Trump announced an immediate 90-day tariff pause for many countries.

At 0631 GMT, the rand traded at 19.25 against the U.S. dollar , about 0.4% stronger than its previous close.

The local currency hit a record low of 19.9325 on Wednesday, the day Trump's country-specific import tariffs kicked in.

Trump said he would temporarily lower tariffs imposed on dozens of countries including South Africa but kept a 10% blanket duty on almost all U.S. imports.

In addition to Trump's tariffs, local politics have also weighed on the risk-sensitive rand, after a contentious budget vote last week threatened the future of the ruling coalition.

The second-biggest party in the coalition, the pro-business Democratic Alliance, voted against key legislation in the budget and challenged the budget process in court.

South Africa-focused investors on Thursday will look out for February domestic manufacturing (ZAMAN=ECI) data due at 1100 GMT for clues on the health of the economy.

Benchmark 2030 government bonds (ZAR2030=) were stronger in early deals, with the yield down 15.5 basis points at 9.15%.

data.title: Any cut in Polish interest rate would harm economy, c.banker Tyrowicz says
data.description:
Inflation in Poland is still high and an interest rate cut would be harmful to the Polish economy, central banker Joanna Tyrowicz told the Dziennik Gazeta Prawna daily in an interview.

Last week, the Monetary Policy Council (MPC) left interest rates unchanged again, but the governor of the National Bank of Poland (NBP), Adam Glapinski, said unexpectedly that the cost of credit could be cut in May.

"In light of the March and numerous previous projections, any cut today would be straying from the (MPC) mandate and would harm the Polish economy," the member of the Monetary Policy Council (MPC) said.

In her opinion, the March projection showed a 60% probability of inflation in 2027 being below 3.5%, the upper band of the inflation target, assuming that the main interest rate remains at 5.75%. In February, inflation was 4.9%.

"I don't know how anyone can think that we have managed to deal with inflation," said Tyrowicz, who has been the only MPC member to support raising interest rates, according to the most recently published voting records.

Her opinion contrasts with those of some other members of the MPC, who see the possibility of starting a cycle of monetary policy easing in Poland.

The National Bank of Poland has kept its main interest rate at 5.75% since October 2023.

data.description:
Czech consumer prices came in at 2.7% on a yearly basis in March, putting the month-on-month inflation rate at 0.1%, statistics office data showed on Thursday.

The year-on-year inflation rate remained stable after a reading of 2.7% in February.

data.title: Hyterra Says Two Wells in Drilling Sequence at Kansas Project
data.description:
Hyterra reported two firm wells are in drilling sequence for the Nemaha project in Kansas, which is prospective for natural hydrogen and helium, as it takes its first steps in executing a 12-month exploration work program, according to a Thursday Australian bourse filing.

Murfin Drilling is on track to mobilize Rig 116 to the Sue Duroche 3 well location early next week.

The initial results of an aerial gravity-magnetic survey support its increased understanding of hydrogen and helium prospectivity for an upcoming exploration stage two drilling program.

Its shares rose over 11% on market close.
